title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Diagnostic Tests -- Complete Blood Count (CBC)
content: Neutrophils (normal value: 1.8 to 7.8 x 103/mm^3) Lymphocytes (normal value: 0.7 to 4.5 x 103/mm^3) Monocytes (normal value: 0.1 to 1.0 x 103/mm^3) Eosinophils (normal value: 0 to 0.4 x103/mm^3) Basophils (normal value: 0 to 0.2 x 103/mm^3) Platelet Count: The number of platelets per volume of blood (normal value: 150 to 400 x 109/mm^3) Reticulocytes: The number of immature RBCs in circulating blood (normal value: 1% of total RBC count)

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Definition/Introduction
content: This reference describes each component of the CBC and discusses the interpretation of results. Though commonly utilized reference intervals are provided, all are lab-specific in practice. The local laboratory guidelines apply when interpreting a CBC.

title: Neutrophilia -- Introduction
content: An absolute neutrophil count (ANC), defined as the percent of neutrophils in the bloodstream and adults, typically ranges between 2500 to 7000 neutrophils/microL. An increase in the WBC count of more than 11,000 cells/microL is defined as leukocytosis. Neutrophilia is the most common type of leukocytosis. It is defined as an increase in the absolute neutrophil count of approximately more than 7700 neutrophils/microL (11,000 cells/microL x 70 percent), i.e., two standard deviations above the mean.

title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Diagnostic Tests -- Complete Blood Count (CBC)
content: Common terms in CBC and their normal values are as follows Red Blood Cell (RBC) Count: The number of RBCs per volume of blood (normal value: 4.2 to 6.9 x 109/mm3 Hemoglobin (Hb): Amount of oxygen-carrying capacity of blood (normal value: 130 to 180 g/L in males, 120 to 160 g/L in females) Hematocrit (Hct): Percentage of whole blood occupied by packed RBCs (normal value: 45% to 62% in males, 37% to 48% in females) Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV): The measure of RBC size (normal value: 80 to 100 micromillimeter) Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH): Amount of oxygen-carrying hemoglobin inside RBCs (normal value: 27 to 32 pg/cell) Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC): Average concentration of Hb inside RBCs (normal value: 32% to 36%) White Blood Cell (WBC) Count: The number of WBCs per volume of blood (normal value: 4.3 to 11 x 109/mm3) WBC differential:

title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation -- Age-appropriate values for leukocytes are as follows:
content: Newborns: 13,000 to 38,000 cells/µL Babies birth to 2 weeks: 5000 to 20,000 cells/µL Children at 1 year: 6000 to 17,500 cells/µL Children at 10 years: 4500 to 13,500 cells/μL Adults: 4500 to 11,000 cells/μL Pregnant females in the third trimester: 5800 to 13,200 cells/µL

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- White blood cell count
content: Identification of a WBC abnormality is followed by an assessment of the white cell differential to identify which white blood cell types are affected. The differential may be reported as a percentage of the total WBC or as an absolute count for each mature white cell type. [15]

title: Histology, White Blood Cell -- Clinical Significance -- Labs
content: Clinically, the complete blood count (CBC) test measures leukocytes. A CBC is frequently ordered to provide insight into disease processes and includes measurements of the leukocyte red blood cell and platelet totals (see Illustration. Illustration of Red Blood Cell, Platelet, and White Blood Cell). Often associated with the CBC is a differential, which refers to the relative amounts of white blood cell types (ie, neutrophil, lymphocyte, eosinophil, etc) as a percentage of the total number of WBCs. Of note, if a subtype of white blood cells seems to be elevated based on the differential, the actual value of the type of white blood cells should be calculated by multiplying the percentage listed on the differential by the total number of white blood cells. The normal range of white blood cell values is 4,000 to 11,000/µL. Anything below this range is leukopenia and anything above this range qualifies as leukocytosis.

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Review Questions
content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
content: Clinicians evaluate the leukocyte count by collecting a peripheral blood smear through a routine blood draw. The cell count ranges vary depending on age and race. In general, leukocyte counts are significantly higher in infants than in adults. [20] Through adolescence, lymphocytes predominate the peripheral smear. By adulthood, neutrophils are the predominant cell line.

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- White blood cell count
content: WBC, or the absolute WBC, is the number of white blood cells present per blood microliter (μL). The normal reference interval in adults is usually 4500 to 11000 cells/μL. Leukopenia is defined as a low WBC and may be due to reduced production, increased utilization, or increased destruction of white blood cells. A spurious leukopenia may occur due to white blood cell clumping. An elevated white blood cell count is called leukocytosis and may be caused by an inflammatory stressor or myeloproliferative pathology. A spurious leucocytosis may occur in the setting of: Reticulocytosis, where nucleated red blood cells are counted as white blood cells Platelet clumping, where aggregate platelets are counted as white blood cells Fibrin clumps Cryoproteinemia

title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
content: Black African, Middle Eastern, and West Indian descent also influence the WBC count and differential. Patients of these backgrounds can have reduced WBC counts and lower absolute neutrophil counts (ANCs) compared to patients of Hispanic and European descent. [21] Known as benign ethnic neutropenia, clinicians may identify this condition in a patient of African descent with no infectious concerns on history and physical examination with chronically low neutrophils on CBC. Benign ethnic neutropenia is an important consideration when evaluating a patient of African descent, as their presentation of leukocytosis may be within the normal laboratory reference range. However, the value must be elevated compared to their prior CBCs.

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Definition/Introduction
content: The complete blood count (CBC) is one of the most commonly performed laboratory investigations in medicine and provides information regarding circulating blood cell size and quantity. [1] The standard CBC includes a measured red cell count (RBC), hemoglobin (Hb), and hematocrit (Hct), calculated red cell indices, a platelet count, and a white cell count (WBC). Most analyzers now also automatically generate a leucocyte differential.

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Conclusion
content: The components of the CBC can help identify a wide range of pathologies and remain one of the most commonly ordered and useful tests in clinical medicine. While automated analyzers have allowed for fast and accurate results, spurious results may arise in several situations (see Table. 3). Differentiating between true changes and spurious abnormalities can impact clinical decision-making and patient care. Results must be interpreted in the clinical context and according to local laboratory guidelines, as reference intervals are specific to each lab. An examination of a peripheral blood smear is essential in assessing any abnormal CBC result and is recommended.

title: CD4 Count -- Normal and Critical Findings
content: The normal CD4 count range is between 500 and 1400 cells/microliters., taking into consideration laboratory variations.

title: Lymphocytosis -- Evaluation
content: The evaluation of lymphocytosis begins with a detailed history and physical examination. The complete blood count (CBC) and review of the peripheral blood smear (PBS) are essential to start an appropriate workup.

title: Leukocytosis -- Introduction
content: Leukocytosis denotes an age-appropriate rise in the white blood cell (WBC) count. Typically, a WBC count surpassing 11,000 cells/µL in adults is deemed leukocytosis, and a WBC count >100,000 cells/µL is termed hyperleukocytosis. [1] However, the threshold varies with age and pregnancy; for instance, 30,000 cells/µL in adults would be abnormal but within the normal range for a newborn. [2]

title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
content: The precise WBC count can differ across laboratories due to variations in the upper limit of normal and the type of hematology analyzer employed. Hematology analyzers analyze whole blood samples for a CBC and differential, encompassing the RBC count, WBC count, platelet count, hemoglobin, RBC indices, and WBC differentials. These analyzers utilize cytochemistry and fluorescence techniques to distinguish between various WBC types and categorize them as low or high using preset algorithms.

title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
content: A careful examination of flagged blood samples through a peripheral blood smear aids in establishing a manual differential and validating automated differentials' findings. Cryoproteins like cryoglobulins and cryofibrinogen can artificially inflate the WBC count. Hyperleukocytosis can also skew hemoglobin and hematocrit values along with the indices.

title: Neutrophilia -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Neutrophilia is defined as a higher neutrophil count in the blood than the normal reference range of absolute neutrophil count. Neutrophilia can be seen in infections, inflammation, and/or neoplastic processes. Further evaluation is often necessary. An absolute neutrophil count (ANC), defined as the percent of neutrophils in the bloodstream and adults, typically ranges between 2500 to 7000 neutrophils/microL. An increase in the WBC count of more than 11,000 cells/microL is defined as leukocytosis. Neutrophilia is the most common type of leukocytosis and is defined as an increase in the absolute neutrophil count of approximately more than 7700 neutrophils/microL (11,000 cells/microL x 70 percent), i.e., two standard deviations above the mean. This activity reviews the pathophysiology, causes, evaluation, and treatment of neutrophilia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with neutrophilia.

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Lymphocyte count
content: The lymphocyte count is the number of lymphocytes per microliter of blood and is expressed as a percentage of the WBC or as an absolute number. The normal reference interval in adults is usually 20% to 40% of the absolute WBC or 1000 to 4000 cells/µL. Lymphocytosis, which refers to an increase in the lymphocyte count, may be due to viral infection, pertussis, toxoplasmosis, tuberculosis, brucellosis, hyposplenism, or lymphoproliferative disease. Lymphocytopenia, which refers to a decrease in the lymphocyte count, may be due to viral infection, bacterial infection, autoimmune disease (such as systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjogren syndrome, and inflammatory bowel disease), malignancy, myeloproliferative disease, lymphoproliferative disease, primary immunodeficiency diseases or medications. [16] [17]

title: Lymphocytosis -- Introduction
content: Lymphocytosis, defined by an increase in absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) to more than 4000 lymphocytes/microL in adult patients, is a common hematologic abnormality. ALC calculates as the total white blood cell count (WBC) multiplied by the percentage of lymphocytes in the peripheral blood. Different lymphocyte subsets (T cells, B cells, or NK cells) may be increased depending on the particular etiology. Lymphocytes represent around 20 to 40% of WBC. The definition of relative lymphocytosis is an increase in WBC of more than 40% in the presence of a normal absolute white cell count. In this review, we present the most common causes in adult patients, in addition to a general approach to diagnosis and management of frequently encountered etiologies.

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Red blood cell count
content: RBC is the amount of red cells present per unit volume of blood and is expressed in cells per microliter (cells/μL). The normal reference interval is usually 4.6 to 6.2 million cells/μL in adult men and 4.2 to 5.4 million cells/μL in adult women. Unlike Hb, RBC does not accurately measure the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity and is not directly used to diagnose anemia. However, red cell indices are important in evaluating anemia. The RBC may be spuriously low in the setting of red cell agglutination or a sample diluted with intravenous fluid infusion. [4]

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Platelet count
content: The platelet count is the amount of platelets per unit volume of blood and is expressed in cells per microliter of blood or cubic millimeter. The normal reference interval in adults is 150,000 to 400,000/μL or mm 3 . Platelet counts may appear low due to platelet clumping in the presence of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) in blood specimen collection tubes, causing pseudo thrombocytopenia. Repeating the platelet count is recommended when a specimen is collected in a tube containing a different anticoagulant, such as citrate or oxalate. [15]

title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Approach to Interpretation of CBC
content: To understand complete blood count, it is important to understand the process of hemopoiesis. Hemopoiesis involves forming mature blood cells from immature cells and consists of a series of distinctively recognizable cells. The production of RBCs, WBCs, platelets, and lymphocytes are named erythropoiesis, granulopoiesis, thrombopoiesis, and lymphopoiesis, respectively. There is a balance between the production and destruction of cells, and each cell has a normal range of count in normal human blood.

title: Leukocytosis -- Evaluation
content: The initial step for any patient with leukocytosis is obtaining the WBC differential and reviewing prior CBCs for any noticeable trends. The next step is to assess a peripheral smear and perform a manual differential if the automated differential is abnormal. If a concern exists about malignancy, referral to a hematology and oncology specialist is necessary.

title: Neutrophilia -- Evaluation
content: CBC and peripheral blood smear: Evaluation of neutrophilia can be started by reviewing CBC with differential and confirming automated differential with a manual by reviewing peripheral blood smear. A peripheral blood smear review can also help differentiate causes of neutrophilia, as mentioned above in the histopathology section.

title: Laboratory Evaluation of Bone Marrow -- Clinical Significance
content: Peripheral Blood Smear CBC parameters Peripheral blood smear description

title: Still Disease -- Evaluation
content: In almost all patients, inflammatory markers such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are elevated. [4] Hematological findings include leukocytosis, typically exceeding 15,000 cells per microliter with a predominant neutrophil count greater than 80%, normocytic normochromic anemia, and thrombocytosis. These hematological abnormalities can be severe enough to mimic primary hematologic disease, and in some instances, red cell aplasia has been observed. [18]

title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Approach to Interpretation of CBC
content: If a single line is affected, then the approach is different, e.g., low RBCs is called anemia, and a specific approach is followed. Similarly, raised WBCs and/or the presence of different immature cells of white blood cell lineage in the blood are referred to as leukemia.

title: Neutrophilia -- Etiology -- Primary Neutrophilia
content: Chronic Idiopathic Leukocytosis: Given neutrophilia is defined as an ANC of at least two standard deviations above the mean, neutrophil counts can vary considerably among asymptomatic healthy individuals, and 2.5 percent of the population can be considered to have neutrophilia based on the definition mentioned above. Serial evaluations may be required in an average healthy individual with mild neutrophilia to rule out the absence of underlying pathology. Since neutrophil count regulation is also genetically controlled, evaluation of a sibling's or parent s blood count can be helpful in this situation. [7]

title: CD4 Count -- Procedures
content: Laboratory measurement of CD4 count through flow cytometry is reported as CD4 percentages. The absolute CD4 count is obtained by multiplying this percentage with the total measured white blood cell (WBC) count. The absolute CD4 count usually corresponds to the CD4 percentages. A CD4 percentage of greater than 29% corresponds to an absolute count of more than 500 cells/microliters, 14% to 28% corresponds to an absolute count between 200 and 500 cells/microliters, and less than 14% corresponds to an absolute count of fewer than 200 cells/microliters. It is, however, important to note that an increase in WBC will cause an increase in the absolute CD4 count while the CD4 percentage remains the same. CD4 count measurements may also have inter-assay or inter-laboratory variability. [6] [7] [8]

title: Normal and Abnormal Complete Blood Count With Differential -- Issues of Concern -- Neutrophil count
content: The neutrophil count is the number of neutrophils per microliter of blood and is expressed as a percentage of the WBC or, as an absolute number, absolute neutrophil count. The normal reference interval in adults is usually 40% to 60% of the absolute WBC or 1500 to 8000 cells/µL. Neutrophilia, which refers to an increase in the neutrophil count, can be due to infection, trauma, tissue necrosis, physical or emotional stress, smoking, myeloproliferative disease, chronic inflammatory disease (such as inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, and rheumatoid arthritis), vasculitides, chronic hepatitis, diabetic ketoacidosis, acute gout, congenital conditions (such as Down syndrome and hereditary idiopathic neutrophilia), blood loss, hemolytic anemia, obesity, pregnancy, or medications.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
content: Karmustin and Merkaptopurin are both contraindicated in the presence of diseases of white blood cells, indicating that these medications should be avoided in patients who have disorders affecting white blood cell function or count.

title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2781
content: Diseases of white blood cells may be treated with meropenem anhydrous, indicating that this antibiotic can be used therapeutically to manage infections or conditions involving abnormal or compromised white blood cell function.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2674
content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض, and a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي is also clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض. Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical link between this symptom complex and HIV infection, indicating that patients presenting with low white‑blood‑cell counts should be evaluated for underlying HIV disease and related symptomatology.

Both drugs are employed in therapeutic strategies aimed at managing infections or complications affecting white blood cell function, reflecting their role in addressing bacterial involvement in hematologic disorders.

These antibiotics are considered potential therapeutic options for managing infections or disorders that involve white blood cell pathology, highlighting their relevance in clinical decisions aimed at controlling such conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
content: The medical concept “deficiency and other anemia” is mapped to the Arabic term “قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض,” which denotes leukopenia—a condition characterized by a reduced white‑blood‑cell count. This mapping indicates that deficiencies leading to anemia are described using the term leukopenia in Arabic medical terminology, highlighting the relationship between anemia‑related deficiencies and low white‑blood‑cell levels.

title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
content: Mitomycin and mercaptopurine are both contraindicated in diseases of white blood cells, meaning that their use should be avoided in patients who have conditions affecting white blood cell production or function. This precaution reflects the potential for these agents to exacerbate hematologic abnormalities and underscores the importance of evaluating white blood cell status before initiating therapy with either drug.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
content: Diseases of white blood cells represent a group of hematologic disorders in which the normal function or number of leukocytes is impaired. The chemotherapeutic agents mitomycin, karmustin, and merkaptopurin are contraindicated for use in patients with these diseases, meaning that their administration could exacerbate the underlying condition or cause harmful effects. Conversely, meropenem anhydrous, a broad‑spectrum β‑lactam antibiotic, may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat infections that arise in the context of diseases of white blood cells, providing coverage against a wide range of bacterial pathogens that such immunocompromised patients are prone to develop. Careful selection of medications—avoiding the contraindicated agents while considering appropriate antimicrobial therapy such as meropenem anhydrous—is essential for safe and effective management of patients with white‑blood‑cell disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض (low white‑blood‑cell count). In addition, عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين (lysine protein intolerance) has قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض as a manifestation. Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical feature linking the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX and lysine protein intolerance, underscoring its importance in diagnostic assessment and patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1316
content: Mitomycin is contraindicated in patients who have diseases of white blood cells, meaning its use should be avoided in this context. Conversely, meropenem anhydrous may be employed as a therapeutic option for treating diseases of white blood cells, providing a potential treatment alternative for these hematologic conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2410
content: تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى (bloody stool) is clinically associated with فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم (anemia resulting from blood loss). This connection indicates that the presence of hemorrhagic stool may signal ongoing or recent loss of blood, which can lead to a reduction in hemoglobin and red blood cell mass. Consequently, patients who exhibit تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى should be evaluated for فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم, and appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic measures should be taken to identify and control the source of bleeding and to manage the resulting anemia.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1359
content: Meropenem anhydrous is classified within the carbapenem structural class of antibiotics and can be employed in the management of diseases of white blood cells. In contrast, the agents karmustin and merkaptopurin are contraindicated for use in patients with diseases of white blood cells, indicating that these two drugs should be avoided in such conditions. This distinction highlights the importance of selecting appropriate antimicrobial therapy based on the specific disease context and the contraindication profiles of individual medications.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1166
content: Stomach cancer, referred to as “cancer of stomach” and also named سرطان المعدة in Arabic, is clinically associated with a low white‑blood‑cell count (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). This leukopenia is further linked to a broader SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX and is commonly observed in patients who have a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي). Consequently, leukopenia serves as a shared clinical finding that connects gastrointestinal malignancy, HIV infection, and a constellation of signs and symptoms, underscoring its importance for diagnosis, monitoring, and integrated patient care.

content: A positive HIV test result (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي) is clinically associated with leukopenia (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
content: support to immune‑modulating virotherapy—used for the treatment of hematologic malignancies such as leukemia and other cancers, while also identifying specific contraindications and adjunctive uses.

title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1121
content: Amfotericin B is classified as a member of the group “Antibiotics for systemic use,” indicating that it is administered systemically to treat infections. Within this therapeutic category, systemic antibiotics also encompass tuberculostatic agents, which are used specifically against Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Amfotericin B may treat diseases of white blood cells, suggesting efficacy in conditions that involve leukocyte pathology. Meropenem anhydrous, another agent used systemically, belongs to the structural class of carbapenems, a subgroup of β‑lactam antibiotics known for broad‑spectrum activity. Like amfotericin B, meropenem anhydrous may also be employed to treat diseases of white blood cells. Thus, both amfotericin B and meropenem anhydrous are systemic antimicrobial drugs, each belonging to distinct structural classes (polyene antifungal versus carbapenem β‑lactam) but sharing the therapeutic indication of managing disorders affecting white blood cells, while the broader category of systemic antibiotics further includes tuberculostatic medications for tuberculosis treatment.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2820
content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS are classified as subprocedures within the broader categories of فصادة الكريات البيض (white‑blood‑cell apheresis) and نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion). Both parent procedures involve the manipulation, collection, or reinfusion of blood components, and the inclusion of INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS under these headings underscores their role in delivering therapeutic agents or devices directly into the patient’s circulatory system or tissues as part of blood‑related therapeutic strategies.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1384
content: Amfotericin B is indicated for the treatment of diseases of white blood cells, reflecting its therapeutic activity against disorders affecting these cells. Meropenem anhydrous is classified within the carbapenem structural class of antibiotics, a group known for broad‑spectrum antibacterial properties. In addition to its classification, meropenem anhydrous may also be used to treat diseases of white blood cells, suggesting that both amfotericin B and meropenem anhydrous have potential utility in managing conditions that involve white blood cell pathology. Recognizing the overlapping therapeutic roles and distinct chemical class of meropenem anhydrous can aid clinicians in selecting appropriate antimicrobial strategies for patients with white blood cell–related infections.

content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
content: Ciprofloxacin, a broad‑spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, may be contraindicated or its use may be avoided in the presence of an infection caused by unicellular organisms (عدوى وحيدات الخلية), underscoring the need to consider the specific type of pathogen when selecting antimicrobial therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

content: how specific autoimmune and hematologic diseases fit within broader disease families, identify pharmacologic options and contraindications for their management, connect advanced nuclear‑medicine diagnostic modalities, and clarify the hierarchical organization of symptoms and immune‑related disorders for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), as the sudden loss of renal function often leads to reduced production of erythropoietin and impaired clearance of uremic toxins, which together contribute to a decrease in red blood cell count. This relationship highlights the importance of monitoring hemoglobin levels in patients with acute renal injury and addressing anemia promptly to improve oxygen delivery, reduce cardiovascular strain, and support overall recovery. Effective management of both conditions is essential for optimizing patient outcomes in the setting of acute kidney dysfunction.
